coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic Hydrogen-Future Energy
coredata.dc:creator: Singh, Arun
Sharma, Neeraj
coredata.dc:description: The rapid increase in energy consumption, driven by the rising vehicle population and travel demand, has led to a surge in greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. This necessitates the development of sustainable and eco-friendly technologies and fuels to mitigate global and local environmental impacts. Four primary justifications support transitioning to alternative fuels and technologies: dwindling oil reserves worldwide, adverse environmental effects of vehicle pollution, escalating oil production costs, and the complex challenges of maintaining energy security in international markets. To address these concerns, there is a growing global demand for hydrogen as a sustainable energy source. Utilizing photocatalytic and photoelectrochemical (PEC) water-splitting reactions, particularly with graphene quantum dots-based composites, offers promising avenues for efficient and eco-friendly hydrogen generation. The selection of these systems is based on their unique physical and chemical properties, with graphene providing strong support and efficient electron transport, while components like cadmium sulfide (CdS) and zinc oxide (ZnO) enhance light absorption and charge separation. This integrated approach shows potential for efficient and sustainable hydrogen production, further bolstered by advances in metal-containing photocatalysts and collaborative efforts across energy markets.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid increase in energy consumption, driven by the rising vehicle population and travel demand, has led to a surge in greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. This necessitates the development of sustainable and eco-friendly technologies and fuels to mitigate global and local environmental impacts. Four primary justifications support transitioning to alternative fuels and technologies: dwindling oil reserves worldwide, adverse environmental effects of vehicle pollution, escalating oil production costs, and the complex challenges of maintaining energy security in international markets. To address these concerns, there is a growing global demand for hydrogen as a sustainable energy source. Utilizing photocatalytic and photoelectrochemical (PEC) water-splitting reactions, particularly with graphene quantum dots-based composites, offers promising avenues for efficient and eco-friendly hydrogen generation. The selection of these systems is based on their unique physical and chemical properties, with graphene providing strong support and efficient electron transport, while components like cadmium sulfide (CdS) and zinc oxide (ZnO) enhance light absorption and charge separation. This integrated approach shows potential for efficient and sustainable hydrogen production, further bolstered by advances in metal-containing photocatalysts and collaborative efforts across energy markets.

ce:section.ce:para: Since India is currently undergoing the process of growing into a developed country, an increase in the demand for greener fuel for the production of power is anticipated. Hydrogen (H2) is considered one of the finest sources of green energy. The hydrogen fuel cell is a more efficient way to meet the expanding need for electricity. With the escalation in concern for changes in the climate and global warming, H2 evolved as an alternative fuel. H2 energy is a viable alternative fuel for automobiles in three different technological configurations: internal combustion engines mechanically linked to conventional vehicles, fuel cells that generate electricity to power electric vehicles, and hybrids that include engine and fuel cell combinations with electrical storage systems, such as batteries. However cost-effective production and storage continue to be the foremost challenges in scaled-up production of this clean fuel. Researchers consider that H2 could be generated from low-cost starting materials if facilitated with specific catalysts. The development of cost-effective cleaner fuels is challenged due to the issues of energy storage and the dependence on the thoroughly accessible feedstock as a carbon source. Employing H2 as an energy resource would lead to the diversification of energy sources and the reduction of pollution and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions in the course of the conversion of energy [1,2]. This review discusses the efficient photocatalytic production of H2 for green energy by utilizing graphene based composite quantum dots and other potential materials. Since H2 as an energy source is a clean fuel, that can be stored for several months in gas cylinders and can be easily delivered to its end users, it has been ranked as a substitute fuel to meet the current and upcoming energy requirements [3,4]. Hydrogen gas has numerous applications, including the generation of electricity, the production of fuel for automobiles and rockets, the use of gas balloons for environmental monitoring, the production of chemicals (ammonia), reducing agents in chemical reactions, and antioxidants. H2 is an indispensable chemical for industrial use in an array of applications, with the petroleum refining industry as the major end user. H2 has the highest energy content of 120.7 MJ/kg which is 2.6 times higher than gasoline. But when compared with volume it has relatively low energy content. Even though burning H2 does not produce GHG emissions, the high-temperature ignition leads to the formation of NOx. As a fuel, the H2 has the benefit that it can be employed promptly in the prevailing internal combustion engines and turbines. It can also be used to generate electricity in the form of fuel cells. The uses of H2, apart from industrial use, include transport applications [5–7], power generation, and heat. In comparison to other options, H2 application in the field of transportation seems to be more advantageous due to the possibility of its onboard storage. Table .1 Shows the Properties of Hydrogen against other conventional fuels. Fig. 1 . Shows the Hydrogen life cycle. Fig. 2 shows a hydrogen gas fuel station.

Commercial steam reforming has been the principal method of producing H2 from the chemical sector. This method is quite expensive and requires a substantial quantity of thermal energy to perform efficiently. In terms of hydrogen production currently used methods [5,6] are (a) Biomass Gasification, (b) Coal Gasification, (c) Steam Methane Reforming of Natural Gas, (d) Electrolysis of Water, (e) Biomass Pyrolysis. Still, for hydrogen production, these methods use natural gases, petroleum coke, coals, biomass, petroleum residue, and other non-renewable resources. Although electrolysis of water uses electricity, it increases hydrogen production costs and indirectly depends upon non-renewable resources [8,9]. Hence another alternative and cost-effective method is required for large-scale hydrogen production. The Photocatalyst's production of hydrogen through water splitting in the presence of light could provide one of the best options [10]. Mainly three methods, i.e. photo-electrochemical, photocatalysis, and photo-biological, have been recognized as the sustainable, renewable, and cleaner routes for the production of hydrogen by splitting the water molecule. Out of these, photocatalytic water splitting is observed to be the simplest route, as it employs the naturally available water and solar energy. Several photocatalysts have been developed using a range of assumptions and methodologies. These photocatalysts have the potential to aid in the production of cleaner H2 via the water-splitting process.

The photocatalysts based on Quantum Dots (QDs) composites, that are nanocrystals of zero-dimension i.e., their physical size is smaller than the excitons Bohr radius, are believed to display the best photocatalytic activity and thus hydrogen production, The exceptional properties of QDs help in enhancing the photocatalytic activity and therefore are the reason that they are chosen for augmented H2 generation over other forms of nanomaterials. A few of the significant characteristics of QDs [11] are, (a) Visible-region active, (b) Improved separation of charge and transport, (c) Multiple excitons generation (MEG) owing to quantum confinement. For absorption of each photon of visible light QDs can release up to three electrons in comparison to the single electron by bulk semiconductor materials, (d) The combined effect of quantum confinement and a large intrinsic dipole moment, (e) Luminescent properties (f) Because of the high value of the excitons co-efficient in the visible range of the solar spectrum, (g) Absorption in the visible range can be produced by doping in semiconductors having a large bandgap. The extraordinary characteristics of quantum dots make them exceptional nanomaterial for use in photocatalysis.

The review discusses the developing photocatalysts using graphene quantum dots-based composites exhibiting high photocatalytic efficiency, increased quantum yield, and enhanced stability, in comparison to the prevalent photocatalysts materials, with the feasibility for industrial-scale production, storage, and transportation in an economical and greener way [12]. The current R&D would aim to meet industrial demand by introducing a method for large-scale production of hydrogen in an environment-friendly and economical manner, along with a solution for its storage, transportation, and portability. Graphene-based material can be synthesized by different methods that encompass solvo/hydrothermal methods, microwave-assisted deposition, sol–gel technique, and solution mixing. Hydro/solvothermal techniques are favored for their cost-effectiveness, simplicity, and environmental friendliness, yielding uniformly distributed semiconductor nanomaterials on graphene surfaces. However, these methods necessitate precise control of operating conditions such as temperature, pH, and duration, often employing solvents like methanol, ethanol, or water under elevated pressure and temperature. Sol–gel processes offer the advantages of low cost, high homogeneity, and purity, involving sequential hydrolysis and polycondensation of molecular precursors. Microwave irradiation and ultrasonic synthesis present promising avenues for controlled nanomaterial synthesis, offering control over crystalline growth without high pressure or temperature requirements. Ultrasonic synthesis ensures uniform distribution and interconnection of semiconductors on graphene surfaces through sonication-induced mixing and evaporation. Various semiconductor materials have been harnessed in the development of graphene-based photocatalysts, encompassing metal oxides, metal sulfides, silver/silver halides, salts, and metal-free polymers like graphitic carbon nitride, among others. These materials are integrated into graphene matrices via two primary methods: (a) in-situ crystallization and (b) ex-situ crystallization. In situ crystallization engenders a homogeneous distribution of semiconductors on graphene surfaces; leveraging functional groups on graphene oxide (GO) to regulate semiconductor crystallinity, size, and morphology. This method facilitates direct electron transfer between graphene and semiconductor nanoparticles. Conversely, ex-situ crystallization combines pre-fabricated nanomaterials with graphene, allowing for the co-assembly of nanoparticles with diverse textures, sizes, and shapes. Nonetheless, this approach may suffer from nonuniform distribution and low semiconductor density on graphene surfaces. In 2020, Makhafola et al. synthesized a Graphene Oxide/Metal-Organic Framework (GO/MOF) Hybrid Composite using the hydrothermal method. They investigated its electrocatalytic activity towards the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). The composite exhibited significantly improved HER performance, suggesting its potential for advancing hydrogen technology through enhanced catalytic activity [13]. In 2021, Neeta Gurbani et al. synthesized Graphene oxide/nickel phosphate (GO:NPO) nanocomposites using one-pot in-situ sol-gel techniques with solar energy assistance. They observed the effect of varying GO:NPO ratios on water splitting activity, finding that the GO:NPO composite exhibited exceptional hydrogen generation performance due to optimal active sites and interface characteristics [14]. In 2024, Mengyao Fu et al. synthesized S and N codoped graphene quantum dots (SN-GQDs) decorated on (001)TiO2 composites via the hydrothermal method for photocatalytic hydrogen production. The composite markedly enhanced surface reaction by facilitating hydroxyl radical formation, leading to a 40-fold increase in hydrogen production activity, showcasing the significance of GQDs in promoting surface reactions [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogen production, storage, transportation, and application
ce:section.ce:para: The United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change has received India's Intended Nationally Determined Contribution (INDC) document, to reduce emissions by 33–35 % by 2030 compared to 2005, for $2.5 trillion, and increasing clean energy in electricity generation to 40 % through non-fossil fuel sources. This was completed in 2005. Carbon-neutral energy sources must be created as a first step towards reducing dependency on fossil fuels and lowering local and global CO2 and other pollutant emissions while keeping economic, social, and environmental considerations in mind. With the rapid rise of the Indian economy, so too are the country's energy demands. Energy security is a significant obstacle that requires creative and original solutions. In the coming decades, hydrogen is likely to play a large role in the energy mix, helping India to satisfy it's expanding energy demands while also conserving the environment and maintaining energy security. However, several obstacles must be solved to go from our current economy, which is based on fossil fuels, to an economy based on hydrogen. Currently, natural gas produces 48 % of the hydrogen, followed by oil (30 %), coal (10 %), and water electrolysis (4 %). The production of hydrogen, as well as other kinds of hydrogen energy such as fuel cells, storage, and transportation, is fraught with problems [30,31]. However, cost and performance difficulties remain, and significant work is still required before hydrogen can be regarded as fully competitive. However, such medium-term competitiveness does not appear to be out of reach, which completely explains the growing interest in these technologies and the global legislative support they are receiving. The most prevalent conventional method is steam reforming to generate H2. Steam reforming is currently the method for H2 production at an industrial scale, but it exploits fossil fuels both in the manufacturing process and as a source of heat and this method is critically constrained by its high cost and low efficiency. Furthermore, CO2 is released in this method. As is known, water is the major and abundant source of H2. Consequently, the generation of H2 by effective combination of solar energy and water has enormous capability to meet the current and upcoming worldwide energy demand in a green way. Therefore, much interest has been focused on the splitting of water for the generation of H2 by photocatalytic or Photoelectrochemical (PEC) reactions as these methods are economical, easy, and useful, and have an enormous capacity for additional advancement. Hence the current R&D discusses the photocatalysts using graphene quantum dots-based composites exhibiting high photocatalytic efficiency, increased quantum yield, and enhanced stability, in comparison to the prevalent photocatalysts materials, with the feasibility for industrial-scale production, storage, and transportation in an economical and greener way. For industrial-scale production of H2-fuel [16], powdered photocatalytic water-splitting has several benefits over photoelectrochemical devices. A high surface area for catalyst-water contact and a relatively simple reactor design are among the benefits. To achieve long-term success with photocatalytic water splitting, a material that is both transparent and capable of splitting water into hydrogen and oxygen is required. Several research have described extraordinarily active metal-containing photocatalysts; yet, the bulk of these photocatalysts only performed half-reactions when splitting water with sacrificial reagents. Domen and colleagues discovered Rh 2-yCryO3/GaN: ZnO complexes. Domen and his colleagues found these compounds, which contain noble metals and are the most active catalysts for the full splitting of water using visible light. The development of abundant and ecologically friendly carbon-based photocatalysts offers an economically viable alternative to hydrogen generation. Graphitic carbon nitride (GCN) and graphene oxide (GO) are both graphite variants [17] are capable of splitting water to generate H2, If a sample is irradiated while containing sacrificial reagents. H2-fueled energy systems ought to provide for industrial-scale production and consumption to compete with previously established, advanced, and traditional technologies. There have been several lab-scale researches for developing groundbreaking H2 methods and technologies. The industrial-scale application demands to focus the research on scaling up. Like in commercially established industries, the scaling up has the benefit of reducing per unit cost of generation, storage, and delivery of H2. Presently, H2 is mainly generated using fossil fuel reforming. The prevailing use of fossil fuels for H2 generation [12] and distribution system suffers from two disadvantages: (a) Increased costs for implementation of Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) to control the emissions released during industrial scale generation of H2 using fossil fuels. (b) Although the least expensive way to deliver the industrial scale of H2 is through pipelines, it incurs high preliminary investment for the construction and setup of new pipelines. The overall system cost is increased by the high price of the H2 systems (e.g., fuel cells). As a result, the current concerns are figuring out how to extend the lifespan of systems while also lowering their prices. Even though increasing production on a bigger scale has the potential to dramatically reduce prices, the price of high-pressure containers, which are frequently manufactured from expensive composite materials, remains the fundamental determinant of how much it will cost to store an item. As a result, extensive research and development are presently being performed to minimise the cost of composite materials used for hydrogen storage in high-pressure containers. The increase in the use of geologically available alternatives should be the primary emphasis of any efforts to scale up hydrogen storage. Salt caves appear to bring a range of technological benefits, but other viable alternatives should be examined as well. Alternatives include gas and oil reservoirs, as well as aquifer structures, all of which have the potential to be beneficial depending on their accessibility and compatibility with existing infrastructure. An investigation of the cohesive use of prevailing and novel technologies should be carried out to enable the commercialization of the manufacture, storage, distribution, and application of H2. For the transition to the industrial scale production of H2 [18–20], multigenerational systems can also be employed. The commercial viability of H2 energy systems can be achieved by the unified endeavors from diverse energy markets. Rapid technological developments can facilitate increased market permeation and enhanced exigence for H2 systems [21]. The commercial-level usage of H2 technologies in various industries would reduce the monetary challenges associated with the advancement of the H2 infrastructure.

Hydrogen is a potential replacement for Compressed Natural Gas (CNG) in the energy industry due to properties such as combustibility, reduced combustion latency, and enhanced flame stability. This encompasses both transportation and other energy-related applications. The use of hydrogen gas is common in the reduction of metallic ores. The hydrogen gas cooling system is used for electric generators. Scientists use this gas in weather balloons because it is lightweight and has many other desirable properties. Welding torches, which are used to put metal together, are powered by hydrogen gas, and welding firms use them. Above all are the massive and potential industrial applications of Hydrogen which may boost our future industrial revolution to newer heights. We need to achieve large scale/high-yield and low-cost production process, a method to enhance the storage capacity over a longer period with its portability and transport, and efficient, large-scale utilization of hydrogen as an energy source in various energy fields which would be more helpful and deliverable for industry scale up and rapid commercialization. Ignition. Fig. 3 . Shows the hydrogen production for green energy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Parameters to measure the photocatalysts' efficiency
ce:section.ce:para:
The activity and quantum yield efficiency are used to assess the quality of photocatalytic production, which is affected by experimental conditions such as light intensity and reaction temperature. Although comparing the activities of different systems might be difficult, the photocatalytic production quality is influenced by these two parameters. It is critical to remember that the genuine quantum efficiency should be the internal quantum efficiency (IQE), which is calculated by dividing the number of reacting electrons by the number of absorbed photons [7]. Due to the nature of the system, it is difficult to compute the total number of photons that a dispersed system's particle photocatalysts have taken in due to light dispersion and loss. In general, a thermopile or a-Si photodiode can be utilized to distinguish incident photons generated by specific photocatalyst systems under the same conditions. Because these two types of diodes can detect photons in the same wavelength range, this is conceivable. So, the obtained quantum efficiency is an apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) [22] [–] [25] at a specific monochromatic wavelength. The AQE and IQE of photocatalysts can be calculated as follows.
A Q E =

Parameter the Quantum Yield metric can be used to evaluate a variety of photoactive materials even while working conditions stay constant. The real quantum yield outperforms the previously estimated quantum yield in the vast majority of cases. If both the incident light spectrum and the absorbed light spectrum are known, this difference can be calculated. The total quantity of energy intake is subtracted from the total amount of energy produced to calculate the overall efficacy. Metal oxide (MO) nanomaterials can also be utilized in photocatalytic and PEC H2 production which exhibit superb chemical stability, high specific area, and mechanical flexibility. One of the main failings is that most of the MOs are UV region active and very few are visible region active. Hence, to make them excellent photocatalysts for splitting water under visible radiation, band gap adjustment is very essential. Numerous attempts have been made to make it active in the visible range, including nonmetal doping, metal doping, nonmetal and metal co-doping, single phase material, merging the huge gap semiconductor with low band gap material, and quantum dots sensitization. These are only a few of the ways that have been attempted. It has been seen that there is a substantial improvement in the photocatalytic activity of MOs after being sensitized with QD. Hence, MO with GQD would split the water under visible light. The quantity of gas (hydrogen and oxygen) produced over time is evaluated when determining the efficacy of a water-splitting system. Because moles are used to measure gas, the unit for rate of evolution is the number of moles that occur in a certain period (for example, mol/hour) [26].

Along with the growing depletion of fossil fuels, rising energy consumption has produced an urgent need for clean and renewable energy sources. Solar photovoltaics are already commercially available and have been the subject of substantial research. Regardless, because the system may only be operated during the day, energy storage is essential. Because of the enormous amount of energy (120 MJ/kg), hydrogen is one of the most promising fuels. If hydrogen could be produced cleanly and efficiently, many of the current difficulties in the energy and environmental sectors may be handled. Steam methane reforming is the most common way of creating hydrogen in industrial settings. As demonstrated by reactions 1 and 2, fossil fuel is used to reform methane, resulting in the release of carbon dioxide gas [32].
(1) CH4 + H2O → CO + 3H2 -------
(2) CO + H2O → CO2 + H2 --------
When it comes to environmental considerations, the creation of renewable hydrogen using solar energy is critical because it can also meet energy demands. There are numerous methods for producing hydrogen using solar energy. The process of splitting water into its constituent parts, on the other hand, is the “Holy Grail” of a sustainable hydrogen economy. The water splitting was initially identified in 1972 by researchers at Kanagawa University in Otahama. UV radiation was focused at a TiO2 electrode in this experiment, and hydrogen was generated at the cathode via a reduction process. Oxygen was produced as a result of an oxidation reaction at the anode. The University of Notre Dame undertook photocatalytic water-splitting research in Indiana and Notre Dame. A particulate catalyst distributed in water or another suitable solution is used in the photocatalytic water-splitting process. The catalyst employed in this procedure must be photoactive and able to generate the necessary charge particles for redox reactions to occur. The Gibbs free energy and potential connection, as given in the following equation, can be used to compute the minimal bandgap required to divide water [33].:
ΔG = -nFE
Where the change in Gibbs free energy is ΔG, n is the number of electrons exchanged during a chemical process, E is the bandgap, and F is the Faraday constant. Here, G = 237 kJ/mol, where n = 2 and F = 96,500 C/mol. As a result, “E = 1.23 V″ reflects the lowest potential, and dividing water requires a bandgap of 1.23 eV [34].

Titanium dioxide (TiO2) exists in three phases: rutile, anatase, and brookite, each contributing uniquely to green hydrogen production. Anatase's high surface area and broad light absorption initiate charge separation, while rutile's stability ensures sustained catalytic activity. Brookite's unique structure enhances reactivity, facilitating efficient electron transfer. Together, these phases serve as active sites in photocatalytic reactions, absorbing light, and catalyzing redox processes to generate hydrogen. Understanding their distinct roles is crucial for optimizing TiO2-based photocatalysts for efficient hydrogen production. Fig. 4 depicts the crucial thermodynamic bandgap for water splitting. Solar hydrogen synthesis via direct photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting is the ultimate goal of a hydrogen economy that strives to be sustainable, renewable, and ecologically beneficial. PEC also referred to as Photoelectrochemical water splitting, is a method for producing hydrogen from water using Photoelectrochemical materials and sunshine. These materials immediately split water molecules into hydrogen and oxygen using the light energy at their disposal. In the long term, this potential technical route may result in less, or perhaps zero, greenhouse gas emissions. Even though several studies have been carried out to address the two fundamental difficulties of photo-electrode (PE) materials, namely efficiency, and stability, neither a normal photocell nor a photo reactor were used in the research. The photocell or photoreactor's fundamental criterion is that it must let as much light as possible reach the PE [35–38]. The representation in Fig. 5 demonstrates a mechanism for hydrogen evolution. The inclusion of multiple intermediate energy levels within the band gap, caused by the integration of Ti3+ and TiC shallow trap into the samples, reduced the band gap of TiO2. As a result, a broader spectrum of light was absorbed. Furthermore, the use of graphene and Ti3+ as shallow trap states enhanced electron transport efficiency and permitted efficient charge separation. As holes exit the valence band (VB), electrons enter the conduction band (CB), with some of the electrons migrating to graphene sheets. Methanol molecules would be in charge of consuming the generated holes (CH3OH + h+ → CO2+H2O), whereas electrons would be in charge of reducing H+ (H+ + e− → H2), resulting in the generation of H2, [27–29].

Fig. 6 depicts the process of photocatalytic water splitting that occurs when exposed to sunlight. In Ag/ZnO/Graphene hybrid catalytic material visible light cannot create electron-hole pairs in ZnO, it can cause visual excitation due to ZnO's excellent charge transfer to both Ag and rGO. Photocytinic activity is boosted by preventing holes from recombining with electrons. The synthesis of Ag/ZnO/Graphene hybrid catalytic material involves integrating Ag nanoparticles onto ZnO nanoparticles, followed by the introduction of graphene. The selection of this material is based on Ag's catalytic activity, ZnO's photocatalytic properties, and graphene's supportive matrix. Ag offers a high surface area and unique surface properties, ZnO facilitates efficient light absorption and charge separation, while graphene provides a high surface area and enhances electron transfer. The synergistic combination of these components is expected to yield enhanced catalytic performance for the targeted reaction, making it an ideal choice for catalysis [39].

Photochemical reactions with the organic dye take place when photogenerated charges move to the particle surface. The presence of graphene sheets and Ag nanoparticles that have a surface Plasmon resonance band in the visible region in a composite with ZnO particles increases the light absorption of ZnO particles in the whole wavelength range (300–900 nm). After absorption of the composite to visible light, the photogenerated electrons from ZnO nanoparticles would be transferred to Ag nanoparticles directly or to graphene sheets then Ag nanoparticles [40]. The protons (H+) would then interact with the electrons, resulting in the creation of hydrogen. The presence of Si can enhance effectively the rate of photogenerated electron transfer which contributes to the excellent hydrogen production performance of Ag/ZnO/graphene-TBSCl composite [41]. Fig. 7 Shows the catalytic mechanism of the Ag/ZnO/graphene-TBSCl hybrid system for hydrogen photo-production and MB dye degradation. Table 2 lists the graphene-based Photocatalysts used to produce hydrogen by water splitting. The choice of synthesis method for Photocatalysts can significantly impact their physicochemical and catalytic properties in hydrogen production. Various synthesis methods, such as sol-gel, hydrothermal, chemical vapor deposition (CVD), and microwave-assisted synthesis, among others, result in different material structures, compositions, and properties. For instance, sol-gel synthesis typically yields Photocatalysts with high surface area, uniform pore distribution, and controllable composition, enhancing their catalytic activity. Hydrothermal synthesis, on the other hand, allows for the formation of well-defined crystal structures and controlled morphology, which can improve light absorption and charge separation efficiency, thereby enhancing hydrogen production. Chemical vapor deposition (CVD) enables the growth of thin films or nanostructures with precise control over thickness and orientation, offering tailored catalytic properties. Microwave-assisted synthesis allows for rapid heating and uniform distribution of energy, resulting in reduced synthesis times and potentially enhanced catalytic activity. The physicochemical properties of photocatalysts, such as crystallinity, surface area, morphology, and composition, directly influence their catalytic performance in hydrogen production. Optimizing synthesis methods to tailor these properties can lead to improved photocatalytic efficiency, making hydrogen production more sustainable and economically viable. Therefore, the selection of an appropriate synthesis method is crucial in designing efficient photocatalysts for hydrogen generation applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: New approach over conventional hydrogen production techniques
ce:section.ce:para: The Graphene and its composite based quantum dot photocatalysts could reduce the hydrogen production cost. H2 has a versatile and large field of applications. However, the H2 production industry based on the prevalent steam reforming methods using fossil fuels is relentlessly limited by high cost and low efficiency and the fact that it emits greenhouse gases, which severely affects human health and the environment. Natural gas currently accounts for 48 % of hydrogen production, followed by oil (30 %), coal (10 %), and water electrolysis (4 %). To divide the water molecule into its constituent hydrogen and oxygen atoms, electrolysis is used. The process of separating the atoms of water with an electric current is known as electrolysis (2H2O + electricity = 2H2 + O2). The cathode collects hydrogen, while the anode collects oxygen. Electrolysis is a way to manufacture exceptionally pure hydrogen that can be fueled by renewable energy; however, the expense of the process has made it prohibitively expensive. The cost of creating hydrogen-using electrolysis is currently twelve times that of natural gas and 1.5 times that of fuels per Btu (British Thermal Unit). Producing, storing, and transporting hydrogen are only three of the many challenges associated with using fuel cells and hydrogen as an energy source. Water is an abundant and renewable attractive way to produce hydrogen; therefore, Graphene/Metal Oxide quantum dots can cost-effectively trigger the production, and fulfill the present need of the industry of hydrogen production. Thus, the discussed R&D provides the troubleshooting of this problem of large-scale production, storage, and transportation of H2 in an economical and environment-friendly manner. The current R&D discusses the photocatalysts using graphene quantum dots-based composites exhibiting high photocatalytic efficiency, increased quantum yield, and enhanced stability, in comparison to the prevalent photocatalysts materials, with the feasibility for industrial-scale production, storage, and transportation in an economical and greener way. The photocatalysts would be developed to work in the visible region of electromagnetic radiation. Initially, photo simulators would be utilized at lab scale research and later a system would be designed and developed to replace the simulator with natural light. It has been discussed that the developed system would cut the cost incurred by the photo simulators in using electricity. Development of photocatalysts using graphene quantum dots-based composites exhibiting high photocatalytic efficiency, increased quantum yield, and enhanced stability, in comparison to the prevalent photocatalysts materials, with the feasibility for commercial scale production cleanly and economically. Definitely, the technique of hydrogen production by photocatalysis of water would be able to deliver technology transfer to the industry and the low-cost high yield would stimulate increased large-scale commercialization of the technology. Petroleum refining, the vehicle fuel sector, power production, gas welding, ultraviolet light production, and space project rocket fuel are the businesses with the biggest demand for hydrogen gas. Between 2017 and 2025, India's hydrogen market on an annualized basis is expected to increase at a 6.3 % rate, rising from $50 million to $81 million. Among the prominent companies competing in this industry are Linde India Limited, Praxair India Private Limited, INOX Air Products, Air Liquide India, Bhuruka Gases Limited, Aditya Birla Chemicals (India) Ltd., DCW Limited (DCW), Gujarat Alkalies And Chemicals Limited, GHCL Ltd, and TATA Chemicals Limited.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Few methods of synthesis of photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: This section discusses the few synthesis methods of composites used as photocatalysts. The first method refers to the effective photocatalytic hydrogen generation of the graphene oxide-titanium dioxide nanocomposite under visible light. Graphene oxide (GO) is produced using the Modified Hummers' method. In this procedure, starting with 150 mL of concentrated H2SO4, 5 g of graphite powder, and two and a half grams of NaNO3 are added and stirred with a magnetic stirrer in an ice bath. In addition, this solution is progressively given 15.0 g of KMnO4 while being constantly agitated. Following mixing and 4 h of heating at 35 °C, this solution is diluted with 230 mL of distilled water. The next combination is stirred for an additional 15 min at 98 °C. After making the suspension, 700 mL of extra-distilled water is added, and the mixture is then stirred for 30 min. To finish the reaction 12 mL of 35 % H2O2 is added to the solution and stirred at room temperature for 30 min. After being cleaned with ethanol and water, the finished product is dried for 4h at 40 °C [46–48]. In the standard fabrication procedure of GO–TiO2 nanocomposite a solution A is prepared by dissolving Ti (SO4)2 (2.4 g) in distilled water (20 mL). By combining 0.44 g of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) with 20 mL of distilled water, solution B, another solution, can be made. Solution A is added to solution B while it is being stirred, and while it is still being stirred, various concentrations of GO are added to the solution. The resulting mixture is given another 30 min of stirring before being put into a 50 mL autoclave and heated to one hundred degrees Celsius for 72 h to undergo hydrothermal treatment. The resultant powder is retrieved by centrifugation and then cleaned with water and ethanol. A solution of NaCl diluted with water and ethanol in a 1:1 M ratio is mixed overnight with the previously prepared powders for the ion-exchange operation. The obtained solid was washed with water, rinsed with ethanol, and then desiccated for one day at 60 °C. Furthermore, in the presence of nitrogen, the synthesized sample is calcined for 2 h at 500 °C at a heating rate of 4 °C per minute. Pt is loaded into a Pyrex glass photoreaction cell by a photo deposition procedure that combines GO-TiO2 and H2PtCl6 solutions with MeOH aq. Solution (20 vol%). After 2 h, the photoreaction cell is continually mixed under the illumination of a 500 W high-pressure Hg-lamp. Centrifugation is used to extract the nanocomposite, which is subsequently dehydrated overnight at a temperature of 60 °C [49]. During the process of photocatalytic water splitting, GO is responsible for absorbing visible light. As a consequence of the d-interaction, the excited photo-generated electrons that are produced as a result of anti-bonding are then injected into the TiO2 conduction band (d-orbital). After being moved to the active sites, the excited electrons begin the process of producing hydrogen gas by either reducing water molecules or protons. Systems composed of MWCNTs and TiO2 have yielded results that are comparable to one another [50].

The second Method is discussing about that with the help of visible light photocatalysis, CdS-metal oxide hybrids were able to generate hydrogen and degrade organic dyes in the presence of graphene oxide. Fabrication of nanoparticles of CdS. The CdS nanoparticles (NPs) would be synthesized at room temperature by dissolving 0.5 g of Cd(OOCCH3)2·2H2O in water (25 mL) in a flask that results in a clear solution which would be then heated to 100 °C. The hot solution would be aggressively stirred while thioglycolic acid was added. The resulting mixture would then be kept at 70 °C for 4 h in an autoclave made of Teflon-coated stainless steel. It would next be necessary to gather the finished product, rinse it with ethanol, clean it with distillate water, and vacuum-dry it [43]. Fabrication of nanoparticles of ZnO, The ZnO nanoparticles would be prepared by dissolving 5.5 g Zinc chloride ( ZnCl₂) in distilled water (200 mL) at 90 °C. While gently stirring this solution, NaOH aqueous solution (16 mL, 5 M) is then added dropwise over a time of 10 min. A supernatant dispersion is attained from which the ZnO nanoparticles are obtained by the sedimentation method. The obtained suspension is cleaned using distilled water and the cleansed particles are ultrasonically cured at room temperature for 10 min with 2-propanol. The ZnO nanoparticles are produced by heating the mixture to 250 °C for 5 h in a Teflon-coated stainless steel autoclave after the particles have been separated using centrifugation [51]. Fabrication of graphene oxide (GO), Graphene oxide would be prepared via a modified Hummers technique as discussed earlier. Fabrication of heterostructure of CdS/ZnO, A modified one-pot hydrothermal method would be used to create the heterostructure of CdS and ZnO after dissolving 4 mmol of ZnCl2 in 20 mL of water at 90 °C. The liquid would need to be slowly stirred as NaOH (5 M) solution was added dropwise using this procedure. A white precipitate of Zn(OH)2 starts to form when the pH hits 7. The aqueous solution (15 mL) will then be dissolved with 4 mmol of Cd(OOCCH3)2·2H2O, which also contains the Zn(OH) precipitate. 4.0 mmol of thioglycolic acid will then be added, and the solution will then be stirred for 30 min. The ensuing suspension is heated to 140 °C in an electric oven for 48 h while being baked in a Teflon-coated stainless steel autoclave. The mixture is centrifuged when it has reached room temperature to produce a solid product, which is then cleaned using a water-ethanol mixture. The cleansed sample is finally dried in air for 5 h at 70 °C, resulting in pure CdS/ZnO [45]. Fabrication of heterostructure of CdS/Al
2
O
3, The binary heterostructure of CdS/Al2O3 would also be fabricated by the single-pot hydrothermal technique as discussed above with Al (NO3)3(9H2O) instead of ZnCl2 and aqueous NH3 instead of NaOH solution. CdS/Al2O3 composite further would be utilized to forming composite with GO. CdS would act as sacrificing regent to stimulate hydrogen production. Loading of GO as co-catalyst with CdS/Zinc Oxide photocatalysts, Solid state procedure would be used to load GO as a co-catalyst with CdS/Zinc Oxide (CdS/Zinc Oxide/GO) in which the synthesized CdS/Zinc Oxide (0.2 g) is meticulously crushed for 30 min with as-synthesised GO (1 wt% of) via a mortar and pestle. The justification for crushing the GO and CdS/Zinc Oxide is to create unvarying connection between them. The produced powder would be used in research into the effect of GO on the photocatalytic activity of CdS/Zinc Oxide. When exposed to visible light, the CdS/ZnO/GO system shows the behaviour indicated in An effective shift of electrons, and the band locations of CdS and ZnO are required during the photocatalytic H2 production process in CdS/ZnO/GO. When exposed to visible light, electrons in CdS's valence band (VB) are stimulated to its conduction band (CB); on the other hand, ZnO does not generate any charges since it is not photoactive. Owing to the nearly same CB value of CdS (20.88 eV) and ZnO (20.22 eV) the reductive electrons easily transfer from the CB of the former to the latter. Due to the high charge carrier mobility of GO the reductive electrons further move from the CB of ZnO to GO, thus, reducing the rate of recombination of photogenerated holes and electrons caused by the reductive electrons' transfer onto the sheet of GO. Also, the photocatalytic reaction space is enhanced as the exceptional properties of GO allow the reaction not only on the semiconductor photocatalyst surface but also on the sheet of GO. Ultimately, the H2 is generated by the reaction of the reductive electrons with the adsorbed H+ ions.

The third Method discusses the solvothermal approach to the synthesis of graphene nanosheets embellished with CdS clusters. Graphene nanosheets coated with CdS clusters and visible light can be used to create hydrogen photocatalytically. This method is effective. As previously noted, the manufacture of graphene oxide (GO) in this situation would necessitate the employment of an adapted version of Hummers' method. The typical method of making the composite involves dispersing different amounts of the synthesized GO and 1.6 mmol of Cd(Ac)22H2O (approximately 98.5 %) across 160 mL of DMSO. This cycle is repeated until the composite is complete. Furthermore, after homogenizing the solution with vigorous stirring and sonication, the mixture is placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 12 h. This occurs after the homogeneous solution has been obtained. After the sample has been taken and allowed to reach room temperature, it is centrifuged to separate it. The precipitate is then washed several times with acetone and ethanol to eliminate any remaining residues of DMSO. After that, the recovered specimen is dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h [43].

The selection of the Graphene-CdS system for hydrogen production is based on its unique physico-chemical properties. Graphene serves as a robust support matrix, offering high surface area and excellent electron transport properties. Meanwhile, cadmium sulfide (CdS) nanoparticles exhibit strong light absorption capabilities and efficient charge separation, making them effective photocatalysts. The combination of graphene and CdS in the hybrid system enhances light absorption, promotes charge carrier separation, and provides a stable platform for photocatalytic reactions. This synergistic effect results in improved photocatalytic activity for hydrogen production. The structural, morphological, and chemical properties of the Graphene-CdS system, hold promise for efficient and sustainable hydrogen generation through photocatalysis. When exposed to visible light, the graphene-CdS system undergoes a process that displays charge separation and transfer. This process is depicted in Fig. 8 . Electrons migrate from the VB of CdS to its CB and subsequently to either 1) Pt deposited on the surface of CdS clusters; 2) C-atoms positioned on graphene sheets; or 3) Pt positioned on graphene nanosheets that have been exposed to visible light. H2 is created as a byproduct of the process as a result of the interaction between the electrons and the adsorbed H+ ions. Even though the CB edge of CdS is even more negative than the reduction potential of H+/H2, the rate of H2 synthesis is insignificant. This is because VB holes and CB electrons quickly reunite after being separated by a short process. Using graphene as an electron collector and transporter allows for the effective separation of photo-induced electron-hole pairs. This increases the amount of time charge carriers can store energy. Fig. 9 depicts a schematic of the production of hydrogen via photocatalytic water splitting.

The Indian Institute of Chemical Technology (IICT) in Hyderabad is in charge of designing and constructing a methanol reformer capable of producing 10,000 L of hydrogen per hour and being compatible with a 10 kW fuel cell. Data was obtained after a total of 1000 hours of operation. To exemplify the notion, a methanol reformer capable of producing roughly 50,000 l/h of hydrogen was planned and built based on this knowledge. This reformer was linked to a fuel cell system with a power output of 50 kW. The reformed gas contains roughly 75 % hydrogen, as well as previously mixed water and methanol. The produced gas was further processed to lower the levels of CO2 and CO to less than 10 ppm. This enabled the gas to be utilized in a proton exchange membrane (PEM) fuel cell system, a device that creates energy. The Indian Institute of Chemical Technology in Hyderabad developed bench-scale catalysts for glycerol reformation at temperatures ranging from 500 to 650 °C. Ni/SiO2 (NS), Ni/Alumina Sol [Ni/Al2O3, and Ni/Alumina Plural (NAP) [Ni/Al2O3 were the catalysts used. This was done to collect data on reaction kinetics in order to scale up the reformer. In response to these discoveries, the institute developed, built, and installed a skid-mounted reactor. The catalyst in question had a maximum lifespan of several hours [52,53].

The PEM methanol electrolyser conceived, built, and tested at the SPIC Science Foundation's Centre for Energy Research in Chennai was capable of producing 1 Nm3/hour of hydrogen at an operating temperature of 50–60 °C. The quantity of energy used was around 2.02 kW h/Nm3 of hydrogen generated. The waste hydrogen gas from this electrolyser is reported to include considerable amounts of methanol. This methanol can be removed by circling the electrolyser with a water scavenger and a chiller. This hydrogen emits practically little CO2 or creates almost no CO2. The Central Institute of Mining and Fuel Research in Dhanbad has created a new method for extracting hydrogen from liquid and gaseous hydrocarbons obtained from renewable and fossil sources. Non-thermal plasma reformation is used in this technique. A non-thermal plasma reactor with a capacity of 0.5 L transforms hydrocarbons into a hydrogen-enriched gas mixture at a rate of roughly 12 L per minute. The use of non-thermal plasma to convert methane to hydrogen in a quartz reactor has been researched. The reformation of soybean oil, methanol, and ethanol with the use of non-thermal plasma has been explored using both the normal cylinder fuel reformer and the vortex type reformer. A significant amount of hydrogen was also created by using naphtha. In addition, biodiesel would be utilized in this approach to make hydrogen.

Garima Jaiswal et al [54], NCL-Pune, reported a novel approach for the generation of H2 gas wherein iron (Fe) catalyst was employed to substitute the expensive noble metal catalysts. The method was cost-effective and viable in that they employed the amply accessible alcohols such as amines and N-heterocycles as initial reaction materials, together with the laboratory-synthesized Fe-catalyst. The Fe-catalyst was acquired by thermal pyrolysis of the iron complex by employing an exceptional shell and core structural design in which the shell was composed of oxide while the core was of carbide. An inert reaction atmosphere was created by passing Ar-gas through the tube meant for the reaction. To achieve complete conversion of the initial material refluxing was carried out for the reaction mixture which resulted in H2 production. The Fe-catalyst was demonstrated to perform successfully by employing it to generate H2 gas from various organic transformations. The catalyst was recovered by simply using a magnet and could be reused for additional reactions. The fiscal aspect of hydrogen energy depends on the price of the initial material and the whole procedure. They concluded that the simple reaction of generating H2 from alcohols could be significant as numerous sorts of alcohols are accessible in plenty and can be attained from indigestible biomass such as lignocellulose and some are currently contemplated as left-over in chemical industrial sectors. Thus, there is great potential to convert waste into fuel by generating hydrogen from these sorts of alcohols which would require some modulation of the catalysts [55].

Karthik et al., SRM University [12], Tamilnadu, An NH2-MIL-125(Ti)/rGO Metal-Organic Framework with non-covalently bound photoactivity was produced by a simple wet impregnation process. The visible-light photocatalytic activity was markedly increased and was 9.1 times higher than before. This resulted from an efficient separation of electron-hole pairs caused by a strong contact between MOF and rGO. Furthermore, photocatalysts loaded with 6 % by weight of rGO achieved an apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of 0.66 percent. Contact-mediated metal-free charge carrier separation was used to get the best AQE when compared to noble metal free MOF-based photocatalysts. This strategy resulted in the highest AQE.

Debasmita Kandi et al, Siksha ‘O'Anusandhan University, Bhubaneswar [28], reviewed the improvement of photocatalytic activity introduced by QDs in materials like layered double hydroxides (LDH), metal oxides, carbonaceous materials (g-C3N4, benzene, and benzene like materials). The QDs are the most guaranteed and environment-friendly candidates for photocatalysis of the water molecule due to their ability to garner visible light radiations and exploit the charge carriers which is attributed to their high excitons coefficient and quantum confinement effect and hence, extensive modification of the metal oxides by the quantum dots for the augmentation of the photocatalytic activity is being carried out.

The review reported excellent photocatalytic activity for the carbonaceous materials like g-C3N4, graphene, and benzene-like materials when altered with quantum dots. The fabrication route for QDs-based photocatalyst materials that achieve high visible light activity and lasting stability under persistent solar irradiation is still a challenge. InN/InGaN quantum has shown the maximum PEC activity with Incident Photon to Charge carrier Efficiency (IPCE) of 56 %, but the stability is still questionable in terms of realistic applications. Fig. 10 Shows the Quantum Dot sensitized photocatalysis.

Ashish Yadav et al, IIT Kanpur, A microbial electrolytic cell (MEC) capable of producing hydrogen was demonstrated employing 3D carbon micro pillars carved on nickel (Ni)-graphene oxide (GO) dispersed carbon film. The 3D micro pillars aided in the formation of a biofilm at the anode and provided relatively greater interaction with in situ dispersed electroconductive GO and electrocatalytic Ni nanoparticles (NPs) in the carbon film. The electrode-based MEC created hydrogen at a rate of 4.22 0.21 m3m-3d-1 when tested at 0.8 V. Overall energy and Columbic efficiencies were determined to be 72.2 3.61 % and 93.5 2.81 %, respectively, with total cathodic and H2 recoveries of 98.7 0.99 % and 92.3 2.77 %. According to the researchers, the MEC's substantially higher efficiency was generated by the synergistic interactions of 3D micropillars, Ni NPs, and GO. This shows that the electrode developed could be a viable replacement for the pricey new metal-based electrodes used in MECs to generate hydrogen gas [56].

Bhavana Gupta et al, Indira Gandhi Centre of Atomic Research, Kalpakkam, reviewed all the synthetic techniques for TiO2/RGO nanocomposites and found that concerning H2 production, the hydrothermal method is a more resourceful, efficient, and highly accepted technique. The hydrothermal technique is recognized for its viability and the fabrication of high surface area RGO that interacts strongly with TiO2. Furthermore, the TiO2/RGO nanocomposite is changed to increase both total and visible light activity. The photocatalytic activities of TiO2/RGO nanocomposites and M-TiO2/RGO nanocomposites under UV–vis light irradiation and discovered that both of these nanocomposites had photocatalytic activities that were inferior to TiO2/Pt photocatalysts. According to this review, the interaction between the nanocomposite's components has a substantial impact on hydrogen-generating value. As a result, in the case of the TiO2/RGO nanocomposite, a separate method is required to integrate an appropriate co-catalyst of metal or metal oxide, which would aid in increasing the interaction while preserving the crystallinity of the TiO2 and the morphology of the RGO [11].

Rajiv Ranjan et al, IIT (BHU), Varanasi, [57] reported the fabrication of rGO-supported CdS photocatalysts in which the GO was reduced electrochemically in situ. The catalyst has been reported to exhibit improved activity for the production of hydrogen by Photoelectrochemical dissociation of water. The increased activity has been linked to the creation of a heterojunction at the interface of CdS and rGO as a result of the two chemicals' chemical interaction. This interaction resulted in more band bending, more charge carrier density, less resistance to electronic mobility, and a low rate of recombination.

Guancai Xie et al, National Center for Nanoscience and Technology, Beijing, China [18], presented an in-depth study of the most recent discoveries from the research on graphene-based materials for hydrogen production through the light-induced splitting of water molecules. The graphene fabrication and design with preferred size, composition, edge structure, and dimensionality need to be explored for extreme enhancement in the efficiency of the PEC/photocatalytic H2 evolution. For instance, the conductivity of graphene produced by the CVD method is significantly higher than that of graphene produced by chemically reducing GO, and the scalability of the CVD process is better suited for the production of large-scale photoelectrodes. Multiple excitons generation (MEG) occurs in doped graphene which can enormously augment the efficiency of hydrogen production if applied to the photocatalysis of water. The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have wide-ranging light responses subject to their specific geometry, size, and boundary conditions. In addition to having extraordinarily long heated carrier lifetimes, GQDs stand out for their extraordinary ability to transform low-energy photons into high-energy ones. These properties have been employed to enhance the photovoltage or photodegradation activity of semiconductors used as electron acceptors or photoabsorbers. GQDs have the potential to be useful in the process of photocatalysis for the separation of photogenerated charges and the absorption of light due to their unique features.

In this section, we covered the role that graphene plays in the process of separating hydrogen and oxygen from water. Additionally, it was hypothesized that the powerful synergistic interaction between graphene and semiconductors could be able to improve the separation of charge and transfer characteristics in photo-excited semiconductors as well as open up the graphene zero-band gap. Graphene is a two-dimensional material that is constructed from atoms of carbon arranged in a honeycomb-like pattern. The findings of the researchers suggest that the relevance of faults in carbon nanomaterials may be related to the electrical properties of the materials. Because of these features, the catalytic activity of aided catalysts as well as their electrochemical stability are significantly improved. In addition to describing the photocatalytic reactions, the proposed theoretical calculations to design new Photocatalysts, evaluate catalysts with increased activity, and engineer the electronic structures of the active surface by changing the structure and composition of graphene-based composites. They also proposed using these calculations to engineer the electronic structures of the active surface. They also advocated utilizing these estimates to engineer the active surface's electrical structures. In order to describe the photocatalytic reactions, several different recommendations were made, in addition to using theoretical calculations. Fig. 11 depicts the basics and key processes involved in the synthesis of hydrogen from water via Photoelectrochemical and photocatalytic reactions.

Ji-Eun Lee et al, University of Ulsan, Ulsan, South Korea, Gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) linked to graphene oxide (GO) on zinc oxide nanorods (Au/GO/ZnO hybrids) with high photocatalytic activity were synthesized utilizing a spray-coating method. The scientists showed that the photo-degradation efficiency of methylene blue was 81 % for Au/GO/ZnO, which is much better than the efficiencies reported for ZnO (62 %), and GO/ZnO (71 %). The increased photocatalytic activity was attributed to higher UV-light absorption and electron transport at the interface, both of which were induced by the work function difference between GO nanosheets and Au nanoparticles [58].

Te-Fu Yeh et al, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan, Taiwan [59], demonstrated graphene-based compounds to be guaranteeing materials for the fabrication of metal-free, economical, and green photocatalysts for overall water splitting. They generated quantum dots (NGO-QD) as photocatalysts by doping graphene oxide with nitrogen. It was possible to assume that the QDs were p-n-type photochemical diodes based on the amplitude of the PL emission, which was measured throughout the experiment. The n-domains and the p-domains, which were the key carrier recombination sites, were connected by a bridge formed by the sp2 groups, which functioned as a link between the two. The recombination of predominant charge carriers that was caused by light irradiation of sp2 clusters led to the development of adequate electron-hole pairs at the diode system's semiconductor-water interfaces. These pairs were necessary for the operation of the system. These couplings were established at the diode system's semiconductor-water contacts. This process of splitting of water reaction with absorption of two-photon shows an extraordinary resemblance to biological photosynthesis.

Wenqing Fan et al, Xiamen University, Xiamen, China Nanocomposites of reduced graphene oxide (RGO) and titanium dioxide (TiO2-P25) are extremely powerful photocatalysts. When compared to P25 alone, identical composites made by UV-assisted photocatalytic reduction and hydrazine reduction, or P25-CNT composites, they found that P25/RGO hydrothermal composites with a mass ratio of 1/0.2 were the most efficient photocatalysts for H2 evolution. This was true regardless of the technique used to make the composites. They added that the nanocomposites are reliable and safe for sustained use [60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Developments of characterisation results and discussion
ce:section.ce:para: The development of characterisation,results and photocatalytic properties of hydrogen production are discussed which are essential for understanding the structure-property relationships of photocatalytic materials and optimizing their performance. Characterization techniques such as X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis, and spectroscopic methods provide valuable insights into the structural, morphological, and chemical properties of photocatalysts, which directly influence their catalytic activity. X-ray diffraction (XRD) is commonly used to determine the crystal structure and phase composition of photocatalytic materials. For instance, the presence of well-defined crystal structures, such as anatase or rutile phases of titanium dioxide (TiO), is associated with higher photocatalytic activity due to their favorable surface properties and charge carrier mobility. The identification of crystalline phases through XRD can thus provide insights into the photocatalytic performance of materials. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) are employed to visualize the morphology, particle size distribution, and surface features of photocatalysts. Materials with high surface area and uniform particle size distribution typically exhibit enhanced photocatalytic activity as they provide more active sites for catalytic reactions. Additionally, the presence of specific morphological features, such as nanoscale structures or hierarchical architectures, can facilitate light absorption and charge separation, further enhancing photocatalytic efficiency. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis is used to determine the specific surface area and pore volume of photocatalytic materials. A higher surface area is often associated with increased photocatalytic activity as it allows for greater interaction between the photocatalyst and reactants. Moreover, the presence of mesopores or macropores can enhance mass transport and facilitate the diffusion of reactants and products, leading to improved catalytic performance. Spectroscopic methods, including UV–Vis spectroscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), offer insights into the optical, chemical, and electronic properties of photocatalysts. UV–Vis spectroscopy provides information about the light absorption properties of materials, with photocatalysts exhibiting strong absorption in the UV or visible range being more efficient for hydrogen production under solar irradiation. FTIR analysis allows for the identification of surface functional groups, which can influence the surface reactivity and adsorption-desorption processes during photocatalytic reactions. XPS analysis provides information about the chemical composition and oxidation states of elements, which are crucial for understanding the surface chemistry and charge transfer mechanisms involved in photocatalysis. By correlating characterization results with photocatalytic properties, one can elucidate the underlying mechanisms governing the performance of photocatalytic materials in hydrogen production. For example, materials with well-defined crystal structures, high surface area, uniform morphology, and favorable optical and electronic properties are expected to exhibit superior photocatalytic activity. Moreover, understanding the impact of synthesis parameters, such as temperature, pH, and precursor concentration, on the structural and morphological properties of photocatalysts can aid in the rational design and optimization of materials for enhanced hydrogen production efficiency. The correlation of characterization results with photocatalytic properties is crucial for advancing the development of efficient photocatalysts for hydrogen production. By gaining insights into the structural, morphological, and chemical properties of materials, one can tailor their synthesis and design strategies to optimize photocatalytic performance and contribute to the realization of sustainable energy solutions.

As India progresses towards becoming a developed country, it is expected that demand for cleaner fuels for electricity generation will increase. The hydrogen fuel cell is a superior answer to the problem of supplying rising electricity demand. The key factor driving the growth of the H2 market in India is the rising demand for H2 in end-user industries. This demand is an important component in the growth of the H2 market in India. Furthermore, the use of hydrogen in power generation, as well as strong environmental rules requiring the use of greener energy sources, have aided in the growth of the hydrogen market. The high costs of delivering and storing hydrogen, as well as the constant price of hydrogen, are stifling industry growth. Liquid organic hydrogen carrier (LOHC) technologies, which are employed for hydrogen storage and transport, on the other hand, are expected to improve future market development potential [61].

The current R&D discussed the H2 generation in a green way with the feasibility of producing it at a commercial scale and storing and transporting it most economically. Hence the discussed R&D aims to benefit the industries where H2 is exploited on a large scale. The fields where H2 gas is used are petroleum refinery, Automobile fuel Such as in Hydro Car and Hydro trains, ultraviolet lamps, Gas welding, production of electricity, structural identification, as a reducing agent, gas chromatography, potentiometric and chemical analysis, rocket fuel for space programs. H2 energy propels various industrial fields and its demand is at the commercial level as discussed here [62]. Fig. 12 Shows the hydrogen field of application.

The space programs employ H2 as their trademark fuel since it provides a strong thrust to rockets. The molecular weight of H2 is the lowest and it burns with tremendous intensity. H2 as a fuel is a clean fuel i. e it produces zero-emission and when combined with oxygen, it is said to provide the maximum specific impulse, about the quantity of fuel expended. Thus, H2 is being conceptualized to fuel automobiles as an alternative to petroleum fuels, and hence development of technology is being carried out to substitute petroleum oils-based engines at a commercialized scale. This technology has the main benefit of the absence of air pollution as H2-ignition produces no harmful emissions. Another extensive use of H2 gas is in the petroleum industry for the removal of sulfur content. Also, it is applied in the conversion of paraffin to iso-paraffin under hydro-isomerization and aromatic to cycloalkanes under de-aromatisation. H2 is also employed to obtain short-chain hydrocarbons from long ones by the process of hydrocracking [63–65]. The H2 gas is commonly consumed in the process of gas welding, also described as atomic H2 welding, for generating a high temperature of 4000 °C for melting the metals and thus unifying the broken surfaces. H2 also shields the metal from reacting with atmospheric elements during the welding process at high temperatures. Deuterium arcs, used in lamps, employ deuterium, the isotope of H2, which are utilized to generate UV light by exciting the deuterium atoms (H2) with heat generated by tungsten filament, and these lamps find application in the spectroscopic analysis in labs. H2 exhibits both electro-negative and electro-positive behavior which makes it effective in redox reactions. H2 finds its use in several approaches to chemical analysis. The potentiometric titrations employ H2 as an electrode by steadily passing H2 gas into the reference electrode while maintaining pressure at 1 atm. These potentiometric titrations are readily affected by the level of purity of the H2 gas. The chemistry of several compounds is found to be complex and thus requires varied techniques to examine the atomic and molecular constitution and bonding. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) is one of the methods used to identify the structure of the molecules and it manipulates the properties of the proton for the same. Therefore, the existence of H2 in the biomolecules helps in this objective of structural recognition. H2 is also utilized in gas chromatography [45] for the separation of volatile ingredients from a mixture for classification. H2 isotope is utilized to make heavy water (D2O) which acts as a coolant and nuclear moderator in nuclear reactors. H2 with oxygen forms an extensively used sterilizing agent in clinics and hospitals, known as hydrogen peroxide, H2O2. A fuel cell is a type of electrochemical cell that generates electricity by converting the chemical energy of a fuel, usually hydrogen, and an oxidizing agent, usually oxygen, into electrical energy via two redox processes. This conversion occurs in a fuel cell. A fuel cell generates energy through a chemical reaction. Each fuel cell contains only anode and cathode electrodes. While the term “anode” refers to the positive electrode, the term “cathode” refers to the negative electrode. Electrodes are the locations where the chemical activities that generate electricity take place. Each fuel cell also contains a catalyst, which accelerates the activity at the electrodes, as well as an electrolyte, which transports electrically charged particles from one electrode to the other. Even though hydrogen is their principal fuel, fuel cells require oxygen in addition to hydrogen. One of the most appealing features of fuel cells is their ability to generate power while emitting little pollutants. Water, a non-hazardous byproduct, is formed when large amounts of hydrogen and oxygen required for energy production combine [40].

In conclusion, the escalating global demand for hydrogen as a sustainable energy source necessitates a shift from conventional methods like steam reforming to cleaner alternatives. Photocatalytic and photoelectrochemical (PEC) water-splitting reactions, particularly those utilizing graphene quantum dots-based composites, offer-promising pathways for efficient and eco-friendly hydrogen generation. The choice of the system for producing hydrogen is based on its unique physical and chemical properties. Graphene provides strong matrix support, a large surface area, and an excellent ability to transport electrons. Meanwhile, the effectiveness of the system as a photocatalyst comes from its ability to absorb light well and efficiently separate charges, thanks to components like cadmium sulfide (CdS), ZnO, and other nanoparticles. When these elements are combined in the hybrid system, they enhance light absorption, facilitate the separation of charge carriers, and create a stable environment for photocatalytic reactions. This combined effect leads to improved efficiency in producing hydrogen through photocatalysis. Overall, the system's structure, appearance, and chemical stability show promise for generating hydrogen efficiently and sustainably through photocatalysis. Advances in metal-containing Photocatalysts further enhance the feasibility of large-scale hydrogen production using renewable energy sources. Scaling up these innovative technologies is vital for industrial implementation. Powdered photocatalytic water splitting, coupled with efforts to reduce hydrogen storage costs through material development and exploring geological storage options like salt caves, is essential for economic viability. Collaboration across energy markets and adopting multigenerational systems can expedite commercialization. Hydrogen's potential as a replacement for conventional fuels in transportation and its versatile industrial applications underscore its significance in achieving a sustainable future. By addressing production, storage, distribution, and utilization challenges, hydrogen can emerge as a cornerstone of the green energy transition, propelling industries towards sustainability and fostering a cleaner, brighter future.
